coredata.dc:title: A cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on nanocomposite for lactate determination
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yunsu
Wang, Yuan
Liu, Yongjie
Shi, Lujia
Yang, Dongzhi
coredata.dc:description: For this study, a cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on nanocomposites was designed to detect lactate. This sensor was established by breaking the inner-filter effect (IFE) between blue emission carbon dots (bCDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and activating the dynamic quenching process of red emission quantum dots (rQDs) and Ag+. In this sensor (bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs), the fluorescence of bCDs was quenched by the formation of CDs/AgNPs, and recovered by adding lactate. Subsequently, the quenching of rQDs was triggered by Ag+, which originated from the disintegration of AgNPs. This cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor for lactate detection was established with the LOD (limit of detection) of 0.12 μM. The developed probe was applied to lactate detection in human serum with satisfactory results, indicating that the probe has enormous potential in clinical practice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Lactate
Cascade-triggered
Carbon dots
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of rQDs (red-emission fluorescent quantum dots)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Higher Education Institute of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61901405 , 61905280 ), the project of Science and Technology of Xuzhou ( KC19066 ), Excellent Talents Scientific Research project ( D2019026 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Higher Education Institute of China ( 19KJD350003 ) and Founded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020M671608 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM characterization of bCDs (A). Insert: bCDs solution under daylight (left) and UV-lamp (right). TEM characterization of rQDs (B). Insert: rQDs solution under daylight (left) and UV-lamp (right).
TEM characterization of AgNPs (A) and bCDs/AgNPs (B). The fluorescence spectra of bCDs, bCDs/AgNPs and AgNPs (C). The UV–vis absorption spectra of bCDs, bCDs/AgNPs and AgNPs (D).

The fluorescence spectra of bCDs and bCDs/AgNPs with various concentrations of H2O2 (A). The UV–vis absorption spectra of bCDs/AgNPs with various concentrations of H2O2 (B). The fluorescence spectra of rQDs with Ag+ (0–100 μM) (C) and H2O2 (0–100 μM) (D).
UV–vis absorption spectrum of bCDs/AgNPs and the fluorescence spectrum of bCDs (A). Fluorescence lifetime spectra of bCDs, bCDs+AgNPs, bCDs/AgNPs (B), rQDs and rQDs+Ag+ (C).

Fluorescence spectra of this ratiometric fluorescence probe for H2O2 determination (A). The curve of ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I445/I645) versus H2O2 (B). Insert: calibration curve for H2O2 determination in range of 0–2.0 µM.
Fluorescence spectra of the blank sensor, the sensor with lactate, LOx, and LOx + lactate.

Fluorescence spectra of this ratiometric fluorescence probe for lactate determination (A). The curve of ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I445/I645) versus lactate in range of 0–200 µM (B) and the calibration curve for lactate determination in range of 0–100 µM (C). Selectivity and specificity of lactate detection, the concentrations of lactate and lactate with interferents (except the concentrations of Cu2+and Fe3+ were 5 µM) were 10 µM and 10 + 100 µM, respectively (D).
The preparation procedures of bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs, and the working principle for lactate sensing based on the developed “cascade-triggered” ratiometric fluorescent sensor.
Comparison the analytical performances for H2O2 determination between the reported papers and this work.
Comparison the analytical performances between the reported papers and this work.

head.ce:title: A cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on nanocomposite for lactate determination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 22004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 22004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For this study, a cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on nanocomposites was designed to detect lactate. This sensor was established by breaking the inner-filter effect (IFE) between blue emission carbon dots (bCDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) and activating the dynamic quenching process of red emission quantum dots (rQDs) and Ag+. In this sensor (bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs), the fluorescence of bCDs was quenched by the formation of CDs/AgNPs, and recovered by adding lactate. Subsequently, the quenching of rQDs was triggered by Ag+, which originated from the disintegration of AgNPs. This cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor for lactate detection was established with the LOD (limit of detection) of 0.12 μM. The developed probe was applied to lactate detection in human serum with satisfactory results, indicating that the probe has enormous potential in clinical practice.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancers are the most deadly type of disease, especially in advanced stages [1]. However, cancers that can be detected early have a high survival rate [2]. Therefore, early diagnosis is of great value to improve the survival rate of cancer patients [3]. The diagnostic methods in clinical practice include an ultrasound scan, CT scan, MRI, and needle biopsy [4,5]. Nevertheless, the sensitivity and selectivity of imaging diagnostic are insufficient, and needle biopsy is invasive. With the development of research, the relationships between biomarkers and cancer have been continuously elucidated [6]. Detection of biomarkers in body fluids (such as blood, urine, saliva, and sweat) can provide a safe and effective method for the early diagnosis of cancer [2,7]. Lactate is the final metabolite of highly proliferative tumors. Hyperlactic disease occurs in patients with malignant tumors, such as lung, breast, prostate, and glioma [8,9]. Detection of lactate in blood is a new method for the early diagnosis of cancer. Therefore, it is imperative to design a sensitive, selective, safe, and straightforward method to detect lactate.

The detection methods of lactate have been reported, including colorimetry, electrochemical technology, gas chromatography, ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and fluorescence method [6,10–12]. Among them, fluorescence method is widely used in various fields because of its following advantages: short response time, low cost, simple operation, and high sensitivity [13]. Therefore, the fluorescence method is considered to be one of the most promising analytical tools for detecting lactate. The ratiometric fluorescent probe uses two fluorescent materials with different emission wavelengths as detection signal [14,15]. For the ratiometric fluorescent probe, the two detection signals can avoid the influence of external factors on the test results and improve the sensitivity and accuracy of the fluorescent probe [16]. However, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor applied to lactate detection was rarely reported. Therefore, it is essential to construct a new type of ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of lactate.

Nanomaterials have attracted great interest due to their excellent optical and electrochemical properties, and they have been widely used in fluorescence sensing [16,17]. Such as carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots (QDs), and metal nanoparticles have been extensively applied in the fields of biomedicine and biotechnology [5,18]. CDs were first discovered in 2004 by purifying monolayer nanotubes through electrophoresis tubes [19]. CDs were formed by top-down approaches and bottom-up methods and had advantages of unique fluorescence, chemical, and mechanical properties. QDs were photoluminescent semiconductor nanocrystals composed of hundreds to thousands of atoms [20]. Due to their high quantum yield, optical stability, broad absorption spectrum, and significant and influential Stokes shift, QDs and CDs had been widely used in the field of biomedical science. For example, Lei Zhang et al. prepared a double-response colorimetric fluorescent probe to detect Pb2+ and PO4 3- in biological samples based on in-situ encapsulation of CDs and QDs into a porous organometallic framework with high selectivity and sensitivity [21]. Xiangzhi Song et al. used a fluorescent probe composed of CDs and QDs to detect spermine [22]. Peiyun Jia et al. developed a fluorescent probe using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for Fe3+ and Cro4 2- detection, and it showed good imaging for Fe3+ and Cro4 2- in HeLa cells [5].

Additionally, because silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have a wide UV–visible absorption band that can quench the fluorescence of many fluorophore groups, they have been used to develop fluorescent probes [23,24]. More attractively, the structure of AgNPs can be destroyed by H2O2, which provides a new perspective for constructing the sensing platform [25]. Since H2O2 destroys the structure of AgNPs to form silver ions and quenches the fluorescence of fluorophore groups through the inner-filter effect (IFE), many fluorescence sensing platforms have been constructed for H2O2 or related biomolecules that can metabolize H2O2 detection [10]. Meanwhile, H2O2 can be produced from lactate which is oxidized by lactate oxidase (LOx) [26]. These suggest that lactate detection can be achieved through H2O2 mediation.

In order to improve the performance of the probe and enhance the synergistic effect of nanomaterials, a sensing probe was developed by using multiple nanomaterials. Meiling Liu et al. used CDs as a reducing agent and stabilizer to synthesize the nanocomposites (Au-Ag/C NC) sensor for lactate detection in human serum samples, which improved detection sensitivity [27]. Hongjun Luo et al. made a hybrid system of CdS and AgNPs to collect fluorescence and surface plasmonic resonance (SPS) signals for the biological analysis of H2O2 with high sensitivity and selectivity [28]. These papers have proved that the combined application of a variety of nanomaterials could improve the performance of the probe. Meanwhile, using the advantages of regulation and amplification effects, the cascade reaction could enhance the synergistic effect of multiple nanomaterials in the probe. Weilin Wang et al. developed a nanomedicine fabricated from the gold nanoparticles-decorated metal-organic framework for cascade chemo-chemodynamic cancer therapy with a significant increase of treatment effect [29]. It indicated that introducing a cascade reaction of multiple nanomaterials to develop a ratiometric fluorescent sensor is a good strategy.

A variety of nanomaterials were introduced, including CDs, QDs, and AgNPs, and a ratiometric fluorescent sensor was developed through cascade reaction. In this sensor, blue emission carbon dots (bCDs) and AgNPs would form nanocomposites (bCDs/ AgNPs), and the fluorescence of bCDs would be quenched by the inner-filter effect (IFE) between bCDs and AgNPs. The target lactate would be detected through oxidation reactions, which would produce H2O2. Next, H2O2 broke the IFE between bCDs/ AgNPs, which triggered the release of Ag+ and the fluorescence recovery of bCDs. Then, the free Ag+ cascade triggered the dynamic quenching process of red emission quantum dots (rQDs) and Ag+. In this senor (bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs), the ratio of the bCDs fluorescence to rQDs fluorescence is used to quantify lactate concentration. Finally, the developed cascade triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor is applied to lactate detection in human serum.

Diethylenetriamine, citric acid monohydrate, sodium borohydride, 3-Mercaptopropionic acid, tellurium (Te), silver nitrate and hydrogen peroxide (30 wt% in water) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethyl alcohol absolute and acetone were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium citrate dehydrates and lactate were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cadmium chloride hemipentahydrate and lactic oxidase (LOx, ≥ 80 U mg −1) were also provided by Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The lactate assay kit (NO.:A019-2-1) was purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). The purified water was prepared using Thermo Scientific Ultrapure water filter which purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals without special specification used in this paper were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The human serum samples were friendly supplied by Xuzhou Central Hospital (Xuzhou, China).

The heating stirrer used in this work is a 78-1 Magnetic heating stirrer which bought from Suzhou weier experimental products co., Ltd. (China). The fluorescence spectra were measured on a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer (Agilent, USA) with 380 nm excitation. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime showed in this paper were measured by an fls980 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). The FT-IR spectra and UV–vis spectra were recorded on FTIR-8400S spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and UV-6100 Double beam UV spectrophotometer (Shanghai Mapada Instruments, China), respectively. The transmission electron microscopic images (TEM) of nanomaterial used in this probe were measured by a G2T12 Transmission electron microscope (JEI, USA). The size distribution of nanoparticles was determined by a Nicomp 380ZLS dynamic light scattering (Santa Barbara, USA). The powder X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were collected using an Escalab 250Xi diffractometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

Before the preparation of bCDs/AgNPs, bCDs were pre-synthesized. As our previously work reported, bCDs were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method [30]. Citric acid monohydrate (1.2 g) and diethylenetriamine (600 μL) were dissolved in 20 mL of purified water, and then heated for 6 h at 200 ℃. To purify the reaction products, they were washed by the mixture of ethanol and acetone. Then, bCDs were obtained by low temperature drying and stored at room temperature for further using.

According to papers reported, bCDs/AgNPs was synthesized by one-pot method [28]. Firstly, 1 mg of bCDs was dissolved in 5 mL water and moved to round-bottomed flask. Next, 10 mL (1.2 mM) silver nitrate and 2 mL (30.0 mM) sodium citrate were added. And then, fresh prepared sodium borohydride solution (380 μL, 10 mM) was cautiously added, dropwise. The brown solution was obtained by stirring for 10 min at room temperature under dark, and then bCDs/AgNPs was obtained by the brown solution stored at 4 ℃ for 3 days. The as-prepared bCDs/AgNPs was stored in water for further experiments. As a reference material, AgNPs were synthesized by the same method as bCDs/AgNPs without bCDs adding.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of rQDs (red-emission fluorescent quantum dots)
ce:section.ce:para: rQDs was synthesized according to the previously work with modification [31]. Firstly, 50 mg of Te powder and 100 mg of sodium borohydride were mixed uniformly into 2 mL of deionized water, and the solution was reacted at room temperature until the color of the solution changed to light purple. The light purple solution was stored for further research. Secondly, 210 μL of 3-mercaptopropionic acid and 100 mL of deionized water were mixed, and 230 mg of cadmium chloride was dissolved into the mixed solvent. Subsequently, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 9.0 by NaOH. Then, the air in this mixture was removed by bubbling nitrogen for 30 min through the solution. Then 1 mL of light purple solution which produced in the previous step was quickly added into this mixture, and refluxed at 120 ℃ for 20 h to obtain red solution. Then, rQDs were obtained by vacuum drying for further characterization and research.

For H2O2 determination, the probe was built by bCDs/AgNPs and rQDs mixing. Briefly, 20 μL of bCDs/AgNPs (0.02 mg mL−1, pH = 7.4) and 200 μL of rQDs (0.3 mg mL−1, pH = 7.4) were added into a tube, and stirring for 1 min at room temperature. Then, 50 μL various concentrations of H2O2 (0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500 μM) were added to the tube, too. The mixture was incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min, and then the fluorescence spectra of this mixture were measured for H2O2 detection.

Firstly, 200 μL of the various concentration of lactate (0, 2, 4, 10, 20, 40, 100, 200, 500, 1000 μM) which diluted by phosphate buffer (10 mM, 7.4) were prepared and co-incubated with lactic oxidase (20 U, 10 μL) at 37 ℃ for 90 min. The reacted solutions were added with the prepared probe (200 μL, pH = 7.4), and then co-incubated at 37 ℃ for 15 min. The fluorescence spectra of the products were measured under the excited wavelength of 380 nm. For detection of lactate in human serum, the serum samples were diluted by a factor of 10 using phosphate buffer (10 mM, 7.4), and then measured as described above.

The morphological features of bCDs and rQDs were displayed in Fig. 1A and B, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1A, bCDs had a non-representative circular shape with a diameter of 5–6 nm; the lattice fringes were also observed in the TEM image [30]. Furthermore, the solution of bCDs under daylight was colorless while it showed a blue fluorescence under UV-lamp (insert of Fig. 1A). The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of bCDs were displayed in Fig. S1A. As shown, the absorption peaks at 240 nm and 360 nm were due to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of CC/CO [32]. The prepared bCDs showed a significant emission peak at 450 nm under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. For rQDs, the TEM image displayed a circular shape with a 3–5 nm diameter and was evenly distributed in solution. The solution of rQDs under daylight was a red transparent liquid while it showed a red fluorescence under UV-lamp (insert of Fig. 1B) [31]. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of rQDs were also displayed in Fig. S1B. As shown, rQDs had a wide UV–vis absorption and an emission peak at 645 nm. Thus, the prepared bCDs and rQDs were consistent with those reported in papers.

The TEM characterization of AgNPs and bCDs/AgNPs were illustrated in Fig. 2A and B, respectively. As shown, AgNPs were round with different sizes and evenly distributed in solution. The TEM image of bCDs/AgNPs showed that their sizes were bigger and darker than AgNPs in Fig. 2A, indicating AgNPs were coated and doped by bCDs [23,28]. The sizes of AgNPs and bCDs/AgNPs were 22.3 nm and 33.5 nm, respectively (Fig. S2). The fluorescence spectra in Fig. 2C demonstrated that bCDs were quenched by the formation of bCDs/AgNPs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of bCDs, bCDs/AgNPs and AgNPs also proved the successful preparation of bCDs/AgNPs. In Fig. 2D, bCDs/AgNPs displayed an absorption peak at 240 nm due to the n-π* and π-π* transitions of CC/CO from bCDs, and the absorption peak at 420 nm, which corresponded to the typical SPR absorption of Ag element from AgNPs [33]. The peak at 420 nm in bCDs/AgNPs was stronger than that in AgNPs, and it might be caused by the overlay of the absorption peak at 360 nm in bCDs. The XPS survey spectra of bCDs/AgNPs in Fig. S3 showed the existence of Ag, C, and O elements, which confirmed the successful preparation of bCDs/AgNPs. Additionally, the C1s peak at 284.79 eV belonged to C-C of carbon skeletons, and the O1s peak at 531.29 eV belonged to CO of the oxygen-containing groups. The Ag 3d3/2 peak at 368.13 eV and Ag 3d5/2 peak at 373.13 eV were agreed with the theoretical value of the Ag [28]. As showed in Table S1, the Ag atomic fraction of AgNPs and bCDs/AgNPs were 11.33% and 2.51%, and the C atomic fraction of them were 87.69% and 97.49%, respectively. These changes could be resulting from the carbon dots dropped in AgNPs to form bCDs/AgNPs. Thus, all of the results suggested that bCDs/AgNPs were successfully prepared.

For this study, the researchers designed a composite nanomaterial-bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs, which provided a cascade trigger effect for the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensor. As showed in Scheme 1, bCDs/AgNPs were prepared using bCDs and Ag+ by one-pot synthesis, and the fluorescence of bCDs was quenching with the product formation. This sensor (bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs) was successfully built by adding rQDs. In the presence of H2O2, the sensor could be cascade triggered. Firstly, lactate was oxidized in metabolic activities to produce H2O2. Furthermore, the structure of bCDs/AgNPs was destroyed by H2O2 through chemical etching, resulting in the release of bCDs and Ag+. The fluorescence of bCDs would be recovered in this release step. Additionally, rQDs were triggered by the free Ag+, which quenched the fluorescence of rQDs. Therefore, the fluorescence of bCDs was increased while the fluorescence of rQDs was decreased with the rise of H2O2. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of rQDs and bCDs was used to measure H2O2 and lactate. The probe using the fluorescence intensity ratio to reflect the concentration of lactate, which have a greater range of signal than a single signal of bCDs or rQDs. Therefore, this strategy had an amplification for detection.

The possible mechanism of this developed probe was studied. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence of bCDs was quenched by the formation of bCDs/AgNPs and recovered with adding H2O2, which indicated the structure of bCDs/AgNPs might be destroyed by H2O2 (Fig. S4A). The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3B showed that the absorption peak (at 420 nm) of bCDs/AgNPs was weakened with the increase of H2O2. As showed in Fig. S4B, the colors of bCDs/AgNPs under daylight were changing from yellowish brown to colorless with H2O2 adding. The absorption peak (at 360 nm) of bCDs was showed as the characteristic peak when the concentration of H2O2 was 500 μM. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of bCDs recovered from 16% to approximately 85%. As the TEM images showed in Fig. S5, bCDs/AgNPs had well defined borders (Fig. S5A), which disintegrated after H2O2 adding (Fig. S5B). Meanwhile, the carbon atomic fraction of nanomaterial also decreased from 97.49% to 85.29%, which mean that the bCDs released from bCDs/AgNPs (Table S1). These results indicated that bCDs/AgNPs were destroyed and bCDs were released, which explained the change in fluorescence intensity at 450 nm [10]. According to the design, the signal of rQDs would be triggered after bCDs/AgNPs were etched by H2O2, which were metabolized by lactate. In order to verify that the fluorescence of rQDs was influenced by broken bCDs/AgNPs instead of H2O2, rQDs were incubated with Ag+ and H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3C and D, when mixed with different concentrations of Ag+, the fluorescence of rQDs was quenched and there was no change after mixing with H2O2. As illustrated in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence of rQDs also kept a stable value when mixed with bCDs. The results demonstrated that the signal of rQDs was cascade triggered by the triggered bCDs/AgNPs.

As Fig. S6 showed, when mixed with rQDs or Ag+, the fluorescence value of bCDs also remained stable. The UV–vis absorption peak of bCDs/AgNPs had an overlay with the fluorescence emission peak of bCDs ( Fig. 4A), which indicated that the quenching of bCDs might be caused by photoinduced electron transfer, Förster-resonance-energy transfer, or inner-filter effect (IFE) [34–36]. The fluorescence lifetime of bCDs with or without AgNPs and bCDs/AgNPs in Fig. 4B illustrated no change, which indicated that the quenching of bCDs was not caused by electron or energy transfer but by the IFE [13]. The reason for rQDs quenched by Ag+ was also discovered. The fluorescence lifetime of rQDs has fallen into decay (from 42.26 ns to 34.64 ns) after being mixed with Ag+ (Fig. 3C). The results meant that the quenching of rQDs caused by electron or energy transfer was a dynamic quenching process [37].

To sum up, the possible mechanism for this ratiometric fluorescent probe is the signal of bCDs triggered by H2O2, which is metabolized by lactate, and that cascade triggered the signal of rQDs. The mechanism of bCDs and rQDs quenching may be caused by IFE and electron or energy transfer, respectively.

The reaction conditions of this ratiometric fluorescent probe for H2O2 determination were explored. As shown in Fig. S7, the appropriate time and temperature of bCDs/AgNPs to be etched by H2O2 were 15 min and 37 ℃. Then, the ratio of bCDs/AgNPs to rQDs was optimized. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of rQDs was significantly higher than bCDs when the ratio was 1–10, which was favorable to establish the ratiometric fluorescent sensor. 1–10 was chosen as the optimal ratio, while the 1–20 and 1–50 showed similar results. As exhibited in Fig. S9, the optimal reaction time and temperature of bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs for H2O2 detection were also chosen as 15 min and 37 ℃, which were consistent with the condition of etching.

Then, the developed probe was applied to H2O2 determination. As shown, the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm was increased with the rising concentration of H2O2, while the fluorescence intensity at 645 nm was decreased ( Fig. 5A). In the range of 0–2.0 µM, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I445/I645) and the concentration of H2O2. Fig. 5B exhibited that the calibration curve fits a regression equation of I445/I645 = 0.09413 C + 0.1965 with r = 0.9903 (n = 3, where C is the concentration of H2O2). The limit of detection (LOD) based on 3σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement, and k is the slope of calibration graph) was 0.02 µM, which was comparable or better than most of the existing fluorescent probes for H2O2 ( Table 1) [15,38–41]. These results indicated that the developed sensor could be used to detect the metabolites participating in H2O2-generation reactions. As shown in Fig. 6, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor was not affected by single lactate or lactic oxidase (LOx). The fluorescence intensity at 445 nm was increased with two concurrent substances of LOx and lactate, while the fluorescence intensity at 645 nm was decreased. Meanwhile, the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (I445/I645) correlated with lactate concentration. LOx could oxidize lactate to reduce H2O2, which provided the possibility of lactate determined by the developed sensor.

In order to detect lactate in human serum, the concentration of LOx, the time, and the temperature of co-incubating reaction conditions between LOx and lactate were researched. According to the results in Fig. S10, when the concentration of Lox was 2.0 U, the response value of the ratiometric fluorescent probe reached the maximum. The developed sensor had an optimal response to lactate when the time and temperature of co-incubating reaction conditions were 90 min and 37 ℃. Under the ideal conditions, with the rise of lactate concentration, the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm increased, while the fluorescence intensity at 645 nm decreased ( Fig. 7A). As shown in Fig. 7B, there was a positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I445/I645) and lactate (in the range of 0–200 µM). Further studies confirmed that the fluorescence intensity ratio (I445/I645) had a linear relation with lactate concentration in the range of 0–100 µM. Fig. 7C illustrated that the calibration curve conformed to the regression equation of I445/I645 = 0.00692 C + 0.1458 with r = 0.9918 (n = 3, where C is the concentration of lactate). The LOD of lactate determination was 0.12 µM, comparable to or even superior to the reported method of lactate determination ( Table 2) [26,27,42–48].

The methodology has been validated to ensure the accuracy and reliability of this probe for lactate detection. The results of Table S2 exhibited that the intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the three different concentrations (2, 20, 80 µM) were all within 6.8%, and the accuracies of them were in the range of 89.7–103.6%, which met the requirements of biological samples testing. Furthermore, the selectivity and specificity of the developed probe were verified by analyzed lactate mixed with the nonspecific interferents or oxidizer. As shown in Fig. 7D, when the concentration of lactate (10 µM) was mixed with nonspecific interferents or oxidizer (100 µM, expect the concentrations of Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 5 µM), the interferents (metal ions, amino acid, carbohydrate, BSA, ClO－ and ▪ OH) did not influence the lactate detection. The reason was due to the catalytic specificity of Lox to lactate [26]. As reported, the fluorescence of rQDs can be quenched by Cu2+ [31]. However, the copper and iron elements in human serum are exist in the form of protein not ion, which would not quench the fluorescence of bCDs and rQDs. Therefore, 5 µM of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in specificity test of this probe is enough for explaining the anti-jamming performance. These results indicated that the prepared probe had excellent sensitivity and selectivity for lactate determination and could be used to detect lactate in the human serum sample.

Then, the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe was applied to lactate determination in human serum by a standard curve method. The lactate concentration was 297.92 µM, the recoveries for lactate in human serum samples were between 87.8% and 95.1%, and the RSDs were from 0.4% to 1.1% (Table S3). The unspiked serum samples also detected by commercial kit, which showed concordant results with the developed work (Table S4). These results indicated that the developed cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor had massive potential in the clinical detection of lactate.

To summarize, a cascade-triggered ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on composite nanomaterial (bCDs/AgNPs-rQDs) was developed. The fluorescence intensity of this sensor was changed by lactate, which broke the IFE of bCDs and AgNPs, and the cascaded trigger established a dynamic quenching process of rQDs and Ag+. Then, this proposed probe was applied to lactate detection with LOD of 0.12 µM. After the methodological validation, the developed probe was also used to determine lactate in human serum with successful results. These all indicated the promising potential application of the developed sensor in clinical diagnosis.

Yunsu Ma: Funding acquisition，Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuan Wang: Validation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yongjie Liu: Software, Writing – original draft. Lujia Shi: Investigation, Supervision. Dongzhi Yang: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (61901405, 61905280), the project of Science and Technology of Xuzhou (KC19066), Excellent Talents Scientific Research project (D2019026), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Higher Education Institute of China (19KJD350003) and Founded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671608).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.131295.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

Mikrochim. Acta

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yunsu Ma is an associate professor at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. She was a postdoctoral researcher at Xuzhou Medical University, China since 2019. Her research interests include biological electronics and chemical sensors.
Yuan Wang is a student at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her research interests include biosensors and biological applications.
Yongjie Liu is a student at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her research focuses on synthesis of functional material.
Lujia Shi is a student at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her research interests include biosensors and biological applications.

Dongzhi Yang is a professor at School of Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, China. Her interests is cancer imaging expert with a broad knowledge of molecular imaging, functionalized materials, and cancer theranostics.
